stages.titleLead: Understanding what you're actually dealing with
stages.lede: Foreclosure in Florida is a court-supervised process — meaning it moves through the legal system with defined stages and timelines. Knowing where you are in that process gives you clarity on how much time you have and what options remain open.

items.label: Stage 01
items.title: Missed Payments & Default
items.body: After one or more missed mortgage payments, your lender will send a breach letter and eventually a Notice of Default. This begins the clock but doesn't end your options — this is actually the best time to act.

items.label: Stage 02
items.title: Lis Pendens Filed
items.body: Once your lender files a lis pendens ("lawsuit pending") in Pinellas County court, foreclosure proceedings are formally underway. A public notice is recorded against your property title. You still have time to sell.

items.label: Stage 03
items.title: Lawsuit & Judgment
items.body: Florida is a judicial foreclosure state — your lender must sue you in court and get a final judgment before a sale can happen. This process typically takes months, giving sellers meaningful time to explore a cash sale.

items.label: Stage 04
items.title: Foreclosure Sale Date Set
items.body: After judgment, a sale date is set — often 30 to 60 days out. This is the hard deadline. A cash buyer who can close in 7-14 days becomes especially valuable here. We've helped sellers close after a sale date was already posted.

items.label: Stage 05
items.title: Deficiency Judgments
items.body: If the foreclosure sale price doesn't cover your outstanding loan balance, your lender may pursue the difference — called a deficiency judgment. A cash sale that covers the balance eliminates this risk entirely.

items.label: Know this
items.title: You Have More Time Than You Think
items.body: Florida's judicial foreclosure process is one of the longest in the country. If you're in the early or middle stages, a cash sale is very achievable. Even late-stage sellers have options — reach out and we'll be honest with you about where things stand.

foreclosureLaw.titleLead: What Florida law says — and why it matters for
paragraphs: Florida is a judicial foreclosure state, which is significant: your lender cannot take your home without going through the court system. This process takes time — time that gives you real options, including a cash sale before the gavel falls.

Here are the key legal facts every St Petersburg homeowner facing foreclosure should know. These aren't opinions — they're facts you can verify through official Florida and federal sources linked below.
foreclosureLaw.disclaimer: This is general legal information, not legal advice. Every foreclosure situation is unique. For guidance specific to your case, consult a licensed Florida estate attorney or a HUD-approved housing counselor.

steps.title: Federal law gives you 120 days before foreclosure can begin
steps.body: Under the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau rules, most servicers must wait until you're 120 days delinquent before initiating foreclosure — giving you time to explore options.

steps.title: Florida requires a court judgment before your home can be sold
steps.body: Under Florida Statute 702.101, lenders must file a lawsuit and obtain a final Judgment of foreclosure before a sale can occur. This judicial process typically takes months — often 6–12 months or longer.

steps.title: You have a right of redemption up until the sale is confirmed
steps.body: In Florida, homeowners can redeem their property — pay off the full amount owed — right up until the court confirms the foreclosure sale. A cash sale to us can eliminate the need for redemption entirely.

steps.title: HUD-certified counselors are available for free — we recommend them

tax.lede: Florida has no state inheritance tax — but federal capital gains rules still apply. Here's a general overview to help you plan ahead.
paragraphs: Florida does not have a state inheritance or estate tax, which simplifies the process for most heirs. However, federal estate tax may apply to very large estates, and capital gains tax considerations depend on the property's stepped-up basis at the time of inheritance.

When you inherit property, the IRS typically resets the property's basis to its fair market value at the date of the decedent's death — this is the "stepped-up basis." This often means little to no capital gains tax if you sell the property shortly after inheriting it, since the basis and sale price are close together.

If the property appreciates significantly after inheritance, you may owe capital gains tax on the difference between the stepped-up basis and the eventual sale price. A cash sale that closes quickly can minimize this exposure.
tax.disclaimer: Tax questions are best answered by a qualified CPA or tax attorney familiar with Florida real estate. The information above is general in nature and does not constitute tax advice.

items.q: Do you have to sell your house in a divorce in Florida?
items.a: No — not automatically. In Florida, you can mutually agree to sell, one spouse can buy out the other, or the court can order a sale. Selling is often the cleanest path to a clear financial break, but it's not always required.

items.q: Can one spouse sell the house without the other's consent in Florida?
items.a: In Florida, a sale typically requires mutual agreement or a court order. If one spouse blocks the sale, the court may compel a sale through equitable distribution proceedings under Florida Statute 61.075.

items.q: Can a court force the sale even if one spouse objects?
items.a: Yes. A court can compel a sale if it serves equitable distribution goals and the legal threshold for partition is met. Once ordered, both parties are legally required to cooperate with the sale process.

items.q: What happens to the mortgage when a divorce happens in Florida?
items.a: The mortgage remains the responsibility of whoever's name is on it, regardless of a divorce decree. Selling the home with a cash buyer pays off the mortgage at closing, providing a clean break for both parties without the risk of one spouse defaulting on a shared obligation.

items.q: How quickly can I sell my Florida home during divorce?
items.a: Speed depends on court timelines and whether a cash sale is pursued. We can close in as little as 7 days once both parties agree and title is clear — providing certainty and reducing stress during a difficult time.

items.q: Is it better to sell or keep the house in a divorce?
items.a: There's no one-size-fits-all answer. Selling provides liquidity and a clean break. Keeping the home may make sense if one spouse can afford the mortgage and wants to maintain stability for children. A tax advisor and family law attorney can help you weigh the specific equity and tax implications.

items.q: Is it possible to use a cash offer when there is disagreement between spouses?
items.a: Yes. Cash offers provide a neutral, court-acceptable path when disputes exist. The offer terms must be reviewed by both parties and approved by the court if necessary. We work directly with attorneys to ensure the offer meets all legal requirements.

items.q: What are the typical timelines from filing to cash-close in Florida?
items.a: With mutual agreement and a cash buyer, you can close in 7–21 days. If court orders are required, add several weeks for the legal process. Traditional listings with financing add 60–90 days or more. Court schedules and title checks are the primary variables.

items.q: What local resources can I reference for divorce-related property matters in St. Petersburg?

prose.lede: Equitable distribution is Florida's method of dividing marital property — not necessarily in equal shares, but fairly. This framework governs how home sale proceeds are split during a Florida divorce.
paragraphs: According to Florida Statute 61.075, the court considers factors like the length of the marriage, each spouse's economic circumstances, and contributions to the marital property. This process directly affects sale timing.

If both spouses agree on a sale, you can move forward quickly. If you can't agree, the court may step in — extending the timeline by weeks or months. Working with a cash buyer from the start can simplify the division process and reduce the risk of delays.

We work with divorcing couples across St. Petersburg and Tampa Bay to provide a straightforward cash-sale path that aligns with equitable distribution goals — no commissions, no repairs, no drawn-out listing periods.

courtProcess.lede: A court can order the sale of your marital home when mutual agreement is not possible. This typically happens if one spouse blocks the sale or if both parties can't agree on terms.

items.q: Is it legal to sell a Florida rental with tenants in place?
items.a: Yes — Florida explicitly allows tenant-occupied property sales. The lease follows the property to the new owner, and tenants retain their occupancy rights throughout the transaction. The sale can proceed while tenants continue to live there normally.

items.q: What is the process to sell a rental property with tenants in Florida?
items.a: Contact us for a no-obligation property assessment. We review your lease terms, tenant situation, and property condition. Within 24–48 hours, we present a fair cash offer. Once you accept, we handle all tenant communication, coordinate deposit transfers, and manage closing. We can close in as little as 7 days — you don't deal with showings, repairs, or tenant coordination.

items.q: Can I sell a Florida rental property with tenants for cash?
items.a: Yes — cash offers are available for tenant-occupied properties, often with a faster, smoother closing than traditional sales. Cash buyers don't require financing contingencies, appraisals, or lender-mandated vacant possession. We buy as-is with tenants in place.

items.q: What happens to the lease when the property is sold in Florida?
items.a: The current lease generally survives the sale, and the buyer takes over as landlord under the existing lease terms. Florida law protects tenant occupancy rights — the new owner inherits all landlord responsibilities and the tenant continues living there through the remainder of their lease period.

items.q: What is cash-for-keys and does it speed up the closing?
items.a: Cash-for-keys is a voluntary agreement where a tenant vacates early in exchange for a relocation payment. We may offer tenants this incentive when vacant possession accelerates the close or benefits all parties. This voluntary arrangement benefits everyone — you close faster, tenants receive compensation, and the buyer gets clear possession.

items.q: What happens to the security deposits at closing?
items.a: Security deposits must either transfer to the buyer (with written notice to tenants) or return to tenants with proper accounting. Florida statutes require written notice identifying the new owner and confirming the deposit amount. We coordinate this transfer seamlessly during closing to protect you from post-sale liability.

items.q: What documents should I prepare for a tenant-occupied Florida sale?
items.a: Prepare: current lease agreements, rent payment history, security deposit amounts, any outstanding maintenance requests or disputes, and any existing tenant notices. We guide you through the full documentation process — you don't need to figure it out alone.

items.q: Do buyers care about tenancy when purchasing a rental with tenants in place?
items.a: Investor buyers often view tenant occupancy positively — it means immediate rental income, no lease-up period, and proven tenant demand. We evaluate lease quality and payment history, but we buy properties with tenants regardless of these factors because we're equipped to handle any situation.

prose.lede: Lease terms, deposit handling, and transfer mechanics determine how quickly a sale can close when tenants are present. Here's exactly how it works in Florida.
paragraphs: When you sell a rental property with tenants in Florida, the existing lease doesn't simply disappear — it transfers to the new owner along with all its terms and conditions. The buyer steps into your shoes as landlord, inheriting the current rent amount, lease duration, and security deposit obligations.

Security deposits must be handled carefully at closing. Florida landlord-tenant statutes require that deposits either transfer to the new owner or return to the tenant, with proper documentation and notice. We coordinate this transfer seamlessly, protecting you from post-close liability.

Notice requirements vary based on lease type. Month-to-month tenancies typically require proper notice before showing or selling, while fixed-term leases continue until expiration unless both parties agree otherwise. We navigate these requirements so you can sell without legal complications or tenant disputes.

Cash buyers like us often prefer tenant-occupied properties because they generate immediate rental income. We can close in as little as 7 days, with or without tenants in place, because we buy as-is and handle all tenant coordination directly.

cards.title: Required Disclosures
paragraphs: Florida law requires sellers to disclose material facts about the property's condition and existing tenant arrangements. You must provide buyers with current lease agreements, rent payment history, security deposit amounts, and any outstanding maintenance requests or disputes.

Tenant notification follows specific timelines. When the property sells, you must notify tenants in writing about the new owner's contact information and confirm that their security deposits have transferred.

cards.title: Deposit Handling at Closing
paragraphs: Security deposits create the most common legal complication in tenant-occupied sales. The closing statement must clearly show deposit amounts transferred to the buyer, and written notice must go to each tenant within 30 days of closing.
We coordinate all required notices and deposit transfers as part of our service, ensuring full compliance with Florida landlord-tenant statutes — protecting you from post-sale disputes.

This clean handoff protects you from future tenant issues while ensuring tenants experience no disruption in their housing situation.

items.q: Can you sell a house with a lien on it in Florida for cash?

floodLaw.titleLead: What Florida Law Requires When Selling
floodLaw.lede: Florida has specific disclosure obligations for flood-damaged properties. Understanding these protects you legally — regardless of whether you sell to us or anyone else.
floodLaw.legalNote: This is informational only, not legal advice — consult a Florida real estate attorney for your specific situation.

items.title: Flood Damage Disclosure
items.body: Florida Statute §689.261 requires sellers to disclose whether the property is in a Special Flood Hazard Area and whether flood insurance has been required. Known flood damage must also be disclosed as a material defect under Florida's general disclosure obligations. Failure to disclose can create post-sale liability.

items.title: Mold Disclosure Requirements
items.body: Mold is a known material defect in Florida and must be disclosed if you're aware of it. Florida Statute §404.056 governs indoor air quality. Porous materials visibly moldy after flooding — drywall, insulation, carpet — are generally considered unsalvageable and must be disclosed to buyers.

link.label: FL Dept. of Health — Mold Guidelines →

items.title: Flood Zone & Insurance Disclosure
items.body: If your property is in a FEMA-designated flood zone, buyers using financing will typically be required to carry flood insurance — a cost that affects what they can offer. You must disclose the flood zone designation. Cash buyers like us are not subject to this lender requirement, which is why we can purchase where financed buyers cannot.

femaInsurance.titleLead: Should You File Your Insurance Claim First,
femaInsurance.lede: One of the most common questions we get from flood-damaged homeowners. The answer depends on your situation — here's how to think through it honestly.

cards.title: If You Plan to Repair and Relist
paragraphs: File your insurance claim first. The payout funds repairs — essential if you're going the traditional listing route. Be prepared for a lengthy process: Florida insurance disputes can take 6–18 months, and contractor availability post-storm can extend that further.

Others sell without filing at all, trading the insurance process for speed and certainty. We're happy to discuss which makes more sense for your specific situation.

items.q: Can I sell my house as-is in Florida?
items.a: Yes. Selling a house as-is in Florida is a common and legal option. We buy homes as-is for cash, handling properties in any condition without requiring you to make repairs. We close quickly and transparently, often within 7 days.

items.q: How fast can I sell my house as-is in Florida?
items.a: We can close in as little as 7 days, depending on your schedule and title clearance. Most as-is cash sales complete within 7 to 14 days — far faster than traditional listings which average 30 to 60+ days.

items.q: What is the Florida as-is contract?
items.a: The Florida as-is contract is a standard agreement (using FAR/BAR forms) stating the property is sold in its current condition with no repair obligations for the seller. The buyer accepts the home as it stands. We explain every clause before you sign so there are no surprises.

items.q: Are there hidden fees or costs in a cash sale?
items.a: No hidden fees. There are no agent commissions in a direct cash sale. Standard closing costs such as title and recording fees may apply, but we explain all costs upfront so you know exactly what to expect before you sign anything.

items.q: What steps are involved to close a Florida cash sale?
items.a: The process: 1) Contact us with property details, 2) Receive a cash offer within 24 hours, 3) Review and accept if it works for you, 4) Sign the Florida as-is contract, 5) We coordinate title work with a local title company, 6) Move out on your schedule, 7) Close and receive payment. As fast as 7 days total.

items.q: Do I need a lawyer to sell my home as-is in Florida?
items.a: A lawyer is not required in Florida for a for-sale-by-owner or cash transaction, but legal review can help — especially with disclosures and contract terms. We guide you through the process and ensure you understand each step before committing to anything.

items.q: What happens if the home has HOA issues or liens?
items.a: We address HOA dues and liens during our evaluation. Cash buyers work directly with title companies to resolve these issues before closing — simplifying your responsibilities and ensuring a smooth transaction without delays.

items.q: Is it better to sell as-is or fix the house up first?
items.a: It depends on your priorities. If you need to move quickly, avoid repair costs, and value certainty, selling as-is for cash makes sense. If maximizing sale price is your only goal and you have time and money to invest in repairs, a traditional listing may yield more. For most time-pressured sellers, the speed and simplicity of a cash sale outweigh the price difference.

items.q: How can I compare a cash offer vs. listing with repairs in Florida?
items.a: Compare total costs, timelines, and net proceeds. Cash offers close quickly with no repair expenses or agent commissions. Traditional listings may yield a higher gross price but require repair investment (often $10,000–$50,000+), 5–6% agent commission, and 30–60+ days of uncertainty. Net proceeds are often closer than they appear.

items.q: What is the typical timeline to close a Florida cash sale?
items.a: Most Florida cash sales close within 7 to 14 days. We can close in as little as 7 days if you're ready to move quickly. The timeline depends on your schedule, title clearance, and any HOA or lien coordination needed.

items.q: What local market factors in St. Pete affect as-is cash offers?
items.a: Market demand, neighborhood dynamics, property condition, and proximity to desirable amenities all influence cash offers in St. Pete. Areas with strong buyer demand and waterfront or urban access command higher offers. We analyze recent sales and local trends to ensure your offer is fair and competitive.

items.q: Is Florida a good state to sell a house as-is?
items.a: Yes. Florida has a well-established as-is sale framework using FAR/BAR contracts, a predictable disclosure process, and a strong investor market — particularly in the Tampa Bay region. Local cash buyer demand is high, making as-is sales practical and fast for Florida homeowners.

items.a: No. There are no real estate commissions or hidden fees when selling to us. The cash offer we present is the amount you receive at closing.

items.q: What is a cash home buyer in St. Petersburg?
items.a: A cash home buyer is a person or company that purchases real estate with available funds rather than requiring mortgage financing. This allows for faster closings, no contingencies, and the ability to buy properties in any condition — including as-is.

items.q: What is the typical process to sell to a cash buyer?
items.a: Submit your property details, receive a cash offer, and close quickly if you accept. Our three-step process is designed to be simple and transparent — contact us, receive your offer, and close on your schedule.

items.q: Do you buy inherited or probate properties?
items.a: Yes, we consider inherited and probate properties. We understand these situations can be stressful and time-sensitive, and we're here to help you navigate the sale smoothly.

items.q: How do you determine the cash offer amount?
items.a: We base offers on your property's condition, local market data for your specific St. Petersburg neighborhood, and our straightforward business model. Each property is evaluated individually, and we walk you through our reasoning so you understand every factor.

items.q: Cash home buyers vs. traditional buyers — which is faster?
items.a: Cash home buyers are significantly faster. Traditional buyer transactions typically take 30–60+ days due to mortgage approvals, appraisals, inspections, and negotiations. A cash sale can close in as little as 7 days with no financing contingencies and no repair negotiations.

items.q: Can I compare your offer to a traditional buyer before deciding?
items.a: Yes — we encourage it. Our offer is completely no-obligation, so you can take your time, compare your options, and decide what's best for you. We're confident our offer will be competitive when you factor in the savings on repairs, commissions, and holding costs.

items.q: Which areas do you serve near St. Petersburg?
items.a: We serve St. Petersburg and the broader Tampa Bay area, including Clearwater, Gulfport, Largo, Pinellas Park, Palm Harbor, Dunedin, Tampa, Sarasota, New Port Richey, Hudson, and surrounding communities.
